title: Sub-Agents, Tasks & Handoffs: Build a Multi-Agent Call Flow
lede: One agent can answer the phone — a team of agents can run your whole front office. This guide shows you how to split a single Smart Agent into Instruction Agents, Tasks, decision routers, and Handoffs, so every caller gets routed to exactly the right specialist mid-call, automatically.

steps.title: Meet Your Agent's Team: The Four Transition Types
steps.content:
A single prompt can only stretch so far. When your agent has to greet callers, book visits, answer billing questions, AND switch languages, one giant prompt gets confused. iMash solves this with mid-call transitions — your main agent stays the host, and specialists take over exactly when needed. There are four building blocks:

  • Instruction Agent — swaps the system prompt in place and stays (a "sticky" persona). Same voice, same call, new personality. Perfect for language switches, escalation tone, or sub-menus.
  • Task — a scoped side-mission with its own instructions and tools that returns when finished, optionally handing back a typed result (like a validated address). Perfect for collecting structured data.
  • If / Else & Category — decision routers. The AI evaluates your instructions the moment the caller responds and routes to a destination per outcome. Perfect for intent triage: "booking → address task, billing → billing agent, Spanish → Spanish mode".
  • Handoff — a one-way transfer to a completely different assistant (its own prompt, voice, and tools). Perfect for departments: sales, support, billing.
💡 A good rule of thumb: use an Instruction Agent when you want the same agent to act differently, a Task when you need something done and handed back, and a Handoff when a different agent should own the rest of the call.

images.alt: Node canvas showing a main agent connected to an Instruction Agent, Task, Category router, and Handoff
images.caption: A multi-agent flow on the node canvas: the main Agent, a Category router, an address-collection Task, a Spanish Instruction Agent, and a Handoff to a billing assistant.

images.alt: Node canvas showing a main agent connected to an Instruction Agent, Task, Category router, and Handoff
images.caption: A multi-agent flow on the node canvas: the main Agent, a Category router, an address-collection Task, a Spanish Instruction Agent, and a Handoff to a billing assistant.

steps.title: Open the Sub-Agents Studio
steps.content:
Everything is authored inside one editor. To get there:

  1. In the left sidebar, click Intelligence Center → Smart Agents.
  2. Click the name of the agent you want to expand into a team. The agent editor opens.
  3. Make sure you are in Form view (the Form / Node toggle sits at the top of the editor).
  4. Below the prompt editor, click the Sub Agents / Tasks / Handoffs button. The number in parentheses shows how many transitions the agent already has.

A large window titled Instruction Agents / Tasks / Handoffs opens. The left rail lists Main Agent, Instruction Agents, Tasks, If / Else, Category, and Handoffs, each with a live count.

💡 Prefer building visually? Everything in this window can also be dragged onto the node canvas — see the "Build It on the Node Canvas" step below. Both surfaces edit the same configuration.

images.alt: Agent editor form view with the Sub Agents / Tasks / Handoffs button below the prompt
images.caption: The Sub Agents / Tasks / Handoffs button lives directly below the main prompt in Form view.

images.alt: Agent editor form view with the Sub Agents / Tasks / Handoffs button below the prompt
images.caption: The Sub Agents / Tasks / Handoffs button lives directly below the main prompt in Form view.

steps.title: Configure the Main Agent (the Host of the Call)
steps.content:
The pinned Main Agent item in the left rail is not a sub-agent — it is your assistant's primary persona, surfaced here so you can steer how it hands calls off:

  • Main prompt — the same system prompt as the main editor. Edits here write through when you click Save.
  • Say before transferring — what the main agent says, in its own voice, the instant it transitions out to any Instruction Agent, Task, or Handoff: Stay silent (default), Say a fixed phrase, or Let AI announce it.
  • Allowed transitions — the gate. Pick which Instruction Agents / Tasks / Handoffs the main agent may start on its own. Empty means it can't start any directly — unpicked steps stay reachable only deeper in the flow (for example via a Task's return targets, or your IVR menu, whose targets appear here as read-only · IVR chips).
💡 Power move: select exactly one allowed transition and it runs automatically at the start of the call — its "say on enter" becomes the opener. That's how you make a Category router triage every caller before the main agent says a word.

images.alt: Instruction Agents / Tasks / Handoffs window showing the Main Agent panel
images.caption: The Main Agent panel: main prompt, "Say before transferring", and the Allowed transitions gate. The left rail shows every transition type with a live count.

images.alt: Instruction Agents / Tasks / Handoffs window showing the Main Agent panel
images.caption: The Main Agent panel: main prompt, "Say before transferring", and the Allowed transitions gate. The left rail shows every transition type with a live count.

steps.title: Add an Instruction Agent (Sticky Persona Swap)
steps.content:
An Instruction Agent replaces the system prompt mid-call and keeps everything else — voice, tools, and the conversation so far. Once active, it stays for the rest of the call (or until another transition fires).

  1. In the left rail, click Instruction Agents, then click + Add Instruction Agent.
  2. Name — a unique lowercase key, e.g. spanish_mode. This is how menus and routers reference it.
  3. Label — the friendly name operators see in pickers, e.g. "Spanish Mode".
  4. Description — tells the AI when to switch into this persona, e.g. "Switch into this agent when the caller prefers to continue in Spanish."
  5. Prompt — the full replacement system prompt while this persona is active. CRM variables like {first_name} work here.
  6. Under When activated, choose Say on enter: Stay silent (default — a seamless swap), Say a fixed phrase, or Let AI greet.
  7. Optionally enable Clear history on swap to drop the chat history so the new persona starts fresh.
⚠️ Instruction Agents have no return timer or return targets — they are sticky by design. If you need control to come back automatically, use a Task instead (a Task can even return to an Instruction Agent).

images.alt: Instruction Agent editor with name, label, description and prompt fields
images.caption: A "Spanish Mode" Instruction Agent: unique name, operator label, when-to-switch description, and the replacement prompt.

images.alt: Instruction Agent editor with name, label, description and prompt fields
images.caption: A "Spanish Mode" Instruction Agent: unique name, operator label, when-to-switch description, and the replacement prompt.

steps.title: Add a Task (Do a Job, Return a Typed Result)
steps.content:
A Task is a focused sub-call: it runs with its own instructions and tools, and when it finishes, control returns to a destination you choose — optionally carrying a validated, structured result.

  1. In the left rail, click Tasks, then + Add Task.
  2. Fill in Name (e.g. collect_address), Label, and Description (tells the main agent when to launch this task).
  3. Instructions — the system prompt the model follows only while this task runs.
  4. Under Behavior, tune how the task experiences the call:
    • Chat context policy — how much of the parent conversation it sees: Full chat history, Exclude instructions (default), Summary, or Start fresh.
    • Allow the caller to interrupt — when off, the task finishes speaking before listening.
    • Tool choice — Auto (LLM decides), Required (must call a tool), or None (no tools).
  5. Under Tools & return value, click Attach tools / MCP to give the task its own tools — custom HTTP tools, prebuilt tools, and MCP servers. They don't need to exist on the parent agent.
  6. Expand Return schema (JSON) to define the typed result the task must hand back (a JSON-Schema object, e.g. street/city/state/zip). Click Validate JSON to check it. Leave {} if you don't need a structured result.
  7. Optionally expand Provider overrides (optional) to run this task on a different LLM, TTS voice, or STT — great for putting a cheaper model on simple data collection.

images.alt: Task editor showing identity fields and instructions for an address collection task
images.caption: A "Collect Service Address" Task: identity, description shown to the LLM, and task-only instructions.

images.alt: Task editor showing identity fields and instructions for an address collection task
images.caption: A "Collect Service Address" Task: identity, description shown to the LLM, and task-only instructions.

steps.title: Decide Where a Task Goes When It Finishes
steps.content:
Scroll to the Task's When activated section — this is where the magic of orchestration lives:

  • Say on enter — what the task says the instant it takes over: Stay silent, Say a fixed phrase, or Let AI greet (default).
  • Return to — the destination(s) when the task completes:
    • No targets — the task is sticky: its persona keeps the call.
    • One target — a forced move: the moment the task finishes it always goes there (e.g. Main Agent (parent assistant), another Task, an Instruction Agent, or even a Handoff).
    • Several targets — the AI chooses one of the listed destinations as part of completing, and can't pick anything outside the list.
  • Each return target carries its own handback settings: what is spoken on return (Stay silent / Say a fixed phrase / Let AI speak), Wait before switching (announce → wait → switch, in seconds), Keep context vs Clear sub-agent turns, and optional extra return instructions.
  • Auto-return timer — the task timeout. When on, the task auto-finishes after N minutes and returns; when off, it runs until it completes on its own.
💡 Chained flows are just return targets: point Task A's return at Task B and you get a step-by-step pipeline — collect the address, then confirm the quote, then book the slot.

images.alt: Task When activated section with Say on enter and a forced Return to Main Agent target
images.caption: "When activated": Say on enter, the forced Return-to destination, and per-target handback options.

images.alt: Task When activated section with Say on enter and a forced Return to Main Agent target
images.caption: "When activated": Say on enter, the forced Return-to destination, and per-target handback options.

steps.title: Add a Decision Router (If / Else or Category)
steps.content:
Decision routers are special Tasks that route by what the AI decides, not by a free choice. The AI evaluates your instructions the moment the caller responds and returns exactly one outcome — each outcome maps to a destination.

  1. In the left rail, click Category (classify into named buckets) or If / Else (a true/false branch), then click + Add Category or + Add If / Else.
  2. Fill in Name (e.g. route_intent) and Label. Note there is no editable description — the router runs automatically the moment the user responds, before the agent replies.
  3. Instructions — tell the AI what to decide, e.g. "Decide what the caller needs: booking a new service visit, a billing question, or continuing in Spanish."
  4. Under Categories (or Branches for If / Else), define each outcome:
    • The category value (e.g. new_booking, billing, spanish) — If / Else gives you fixed true/false tags instead.
    • The → destination — any Task, Instruction Agent, or Handoff, or Main Agent, or Stay (no transfer) to make the router itself keep the call.
    • A short "when does this category apply?" hint that helps the AI decide.
  5. Click + Add category for more outcomes (Category mode only).
💡 The return-value schema for a router is built automatically — you never touch JSON for a decision step.

images.alt: Category decision editor with three categories routing to a task, a handoff, and an instruction agent
images.caption: A Category router: new_booking → Collect Service Address (task), billing → Transfer to Billing (handoff), spanish → Spanish Mode (instruction agent).

images.alt: Category decision editor with three categories routing to a task, a handoff, and an instruction agent
images.caption: A Category router: new_booking → Collect Service Address (task), billing → Transfer to Billing (handoff), spanish → Spanish Mode (instruction agent).

steps.title: Add a Handoff (Transfer to Another Assistant)
steps.content:
A Handoff moves the caller to an entirely different assistant from your account — its own prompt, voice, LLM, and tools take over for the rest of the call. This is a one-way door: there is no automatic path back.

  1. In the left rail, click Handoffs, then + Add Handoff.
  2. Fill in Name (e.g. to_billing), Label, and a Description that tells the AI when to hand off ("…when the caller asks about an invoice, a charge or a refund").
  3. Target assistant — pick any other assistant in your account. Self-handoff is rejected.
  4. Carry history — what the next agent knows:
    • Full transcript — it sees every turn so far.
    • Summary (default) — it receives an AI-generated summary.
    • Start fresh — it starts with no prior context.
  5. Say on handoff — an optional one-shot line spoken at the moment of transfer, e.g. "One moment — connecting you to our billing team."
⚠️ Because handoffs never bounce back, use them for true department transfers. If you need the caller returned afterwards, use a Task instead.

images.alt: Handoff editor with target assistant picker and carry history options
images.caption: A "Transfer to Billing" Handoff: destination assistant, carry-history choice, and the handoff line.

images.alt: Handoff editor with target assistant picker and carry history options
images.caption: A "Transfer to Billing" Handoff: destination assistant, carry-history choice, and the handoff line.

steps.title: Wire Transitions to an IVR Menu (Press 1 for…)
steps.content:
Your sub-agents can also be triggered by a classic phone menu — digit presses or spoken keywords:

  1. In the agent editor's right-hand list, click IVR Navigation. The IVR window opens.
  2. In General, switch on Enable IVR Navigation and write your Menu Introduction Text ("Press 1 to book a service visit, press 2 for billing…").
  3. Click Menu Options in the left rail, then + Add Menu Option.
  4. Fill in the Digit (1–9, *, #), a Label, and optional Speech Keywords (comma-separated words that trigger the option when spoken, e.g. "book, appointment, schedule").
  5. Click Add Option, and repeat for each menu entry.

The Menu Options table shows every mapping at a glance: digit, label, target, and keywords.

💡 IVR-triggered targets appear in the Main Agent's Allowed transitions list as purple · IVR chips — they're managed here, so you never have to add them twice.

images.alt: IVR Navigation Menu Options table mapping digits to sub-agent targets
images.caption: Menu Options: digit 1 launches the address Task, digit 2 fires the billing Handoff — each with spoken keywords.

images.alt: IVR Navigation Menu Options table mapping digits to sub-agent targets
images.caption: Menu Options: digit 1 launches the address Task, digit 2 fires the billing Handoff — each with spoken keywords.

steps.title: Pick Targets with the Transition Picker
steps.content:
Wherever a screen asks "where should this go?", you'll meet the same unified picker. In the IVR Add Menu Option window it's the Target field:

  1. Click the Target dropdown (placeholder: Pick an Instruction Agent / Task / Handoff).
  2. Options are grouped by kind — Instruction Agents, Tasks, and Handoffs — each with a colored tag and its description, so you always know what kind of transition you're wiring.
  3. Type to search by name, then click the destination.

The same picker appears in CRM Dispatch settings and anywhere else transitions are referenced — one mental model everywhere.

images.alt: Transition Picker dropdown grouped into Instruction Agents, Tasks and Handoffs
images.caption: The Transition Picker groups every destination by kind, with color-coded tags and descriptions.

images.alt: Transition Picker dropdown grouped into Instruction Agents, Tasks and Handoffs
images.caption: The Transition Picker groups every destination by kind, with color-coded tags and descriptions.

steps.title: Build It on the Node Canvas
steps.content:
Prefer to see your flow? Switch the editor to Node view (the Form / Node toggle) and your whole team renders as a live graph:

  • Every Instruction Agent, Task, decision router, and Handoff is a canvas node; return routes are drawn as dashed edges, and decision outcomes appear as labeled branch edges (e.g. new_booking, billing, spanish).
  • Open the Components palette (the grid icon in the right rail) and drag new blocks from the Agents & Transitions group: Task, If / Else, Category, Instruction Agent, Handoff.
  • Click the gear on any sub-agent node to open its full configuration — the exact same editor you used in the Form-view window.
  • Drag between node handles to create return routes; drag nodes to arrange the flow. Changes auto-save (watch the Saved badge in the bottom bar).
  • You can even ask the AI Builder bar at the bottom to build for you: "Add a Sales task and let Main transition to it".

images.alt: Category node configuration modal opened from the node canvas
images.caption: Click a node's gear to configure it in place — here the Category router, with its branch edges visible behind the window.

images.alt: Category node configuration modal opened from the node canvas
images.caption: Click a node's gear to configure it in place — here the Category router, with its branch edges visible behind the window.

steps.title: Save, Validate, and Test Your Team
steps.content:
The studio validates as you type, so broken flows never reach a caller:

  1. Watch the footer of the Instruction Agents / Tasks / Handoffs window: an Unsaved changes tag appears while editing, and any errors or warnings show as counters — hover them for a per-entry breakdown ("What needs fixing") and click a line to jump straight to the problem.
  2. Click Save. The button stays disabled while errors remain — duplicate names, missing prompts, or a handoff without a target are caught before they cost you a call.
  3. Now put your team on the phone: click Test Agent in the editor header (or Connect in the chat panel) and try lines like "I'd like to book a repair", "I have a question about my invoice", or "¿Puedo continuar en español?" — and watch the right specialist take over, live.

That's the whole play: one number, one call, a whole team of specialists behind it. 🚀

support.title: Need Help?
support.content: Designing a complex call flow and want a second pair of eyes? Email support@imash.io and the iMash team will help you architect your multi-agent setup.

metaDescription: Build a multi-agent call flow in iMash: sticky Instruction Agents, Tasks with typed returns, If/Else and Category decision routers, Handoffs between assistants, IVR wiring, and the node canvas.
